title: Extending garment life through repair
subtitle: Darning, patching, and simple alterations to delay disposal
excerpt: A garment sent to landfill before its usable life is exhausted represents the full carbon and water cost of its production. Simple repairs (darning knits, patching denim, replacing fasteners) extend life by years and require only basic tools.
sourceNotes: WRAP Textiles and the Environment report (2023). Ellen MacArthur Foundation Circular Economy for Fashion (2017). Waste and Resources Action Programme (WRAP) guidance on textile repair and reuse.

content.text: The UK disposes of approximately 350,000 tonnes of clothing in household bins each year. The production of textiles accounts for approximately 10% of global industrial carbon emissions. Extending a garment's active life from 1 year to 2 years halves its annualised
: no other household textile action has a comparable return per hour spent.

Holes in knitted socks, jumpers, and cardigans are darned by weaving replacement yarn across the hole on a domed surface (a wooden darn egg or a smooth rounded object). Use a yarn of similar weight and fibre content. Weave the first layer of parallel strands across the hole and slightly into the intact fabric on each side. Weave the second layer perpendicular to the first. Visible mending with a contrasting colour is a deliberate aesthetic choice; invisible mending matches the base yarn exactly.

Worn knees and seat patches in denim and work trousers: cut a patch 30 mm larger than the worn area in all directions. For an invisible internal patch, iron-on fusible web bonds the patch to the wrong side; for a visible external patch, machine-stitch around the perimeter with a short straight or zigzag stitch. Denim patches on denim are near-invisible when grain is matched.

Buttons: sew replacement buttons using a shank of several loops of thread behind the button for thick fabric. Sew through in a figure-of-eight pattern. Waxed thread resists abrasion better than standard cotton thread.

Zip: replacement by hand stitching is possible but slow. Machine replacement takes 30 minutes with practice. Open-ended jacket zips are harder to match than a standard separating zip; many haberdashers cut to length.
Hook and eye, poppers, and Velcro. You can get push-through replacements at a haberdashers. None of them need sewing.

Machine washing at 30°C instead of 40°C reduces fibre degradation from heat and agitation over hundreds of cycles. Washing less frequently (airing rather than washing after light wear) extends garment life: most fibre damage in domestic clothing comes from the wash cycle, not from wearing. Line drying rather than tumble drying eliminates the main mechanical stress that frays fabric over time. The decisions made at laundry stage compound over the lifetime of a garment more than any single repair.

attrs.body: Repair makes sense when the garment has remaining useful life and the repair is achievable in less time than the garment is worth. A cheap garment worn once is not worth 3 hours of tailoring. A much-used pair of trousers or a warm jumper is. The circular economy principle is to maintain value: repair high-value, high-use garments and accept that fast fashion items are difficult to justify repairing economically.
attrs.title: When repair is not worthwhile
